opus_reasoning: Story centers on a hidden righteous man (R. Shabtai) whose Sabbath joy is revealed by the Besht to his disciples, plus prophecy of the Maggid's birth — fits revelation of a hidden tzaddik.
The Baal Shem Tov supernaturally foreknows the future poverty of the host and the pregnancies/genders of the parnasim's wives, classic ruach hakodesh.
The Baal Shem Tov sits all day in the beit midrash on erev Pesach, making it an actual setting in the story.
The Besht predicts he will reach Berlin by Shabbat and that the child will live—both predictions are fulfilled.
Story features the Besht with disciples and non-Hasidic figures (innkeeper, sorcerer, wedding party); no depiction of interaction among Hasidim as peers.
No study hall setting; story takes place on the road, at an inn, a wedding, cemetery, and a field.
No maggid/preacher character appears; the story features a tzaddik (R. Michel of Zlotchov), a wealthy man, and a soldier.
The rabbi disguises himself to test the innkeeper, but this is a brief incidental disguise, not a deception central to the story; no clear lying act depicted.
Story involves a Jewish tavern-keeper (arendar) and the Rabbi of Apta; no non-Jews are depicted.
The Rebbe of Apta tests the man and claims he can know all his deeds from birth, and the man's blessings are supernaturally fulfilled — implying ruach hakodesh perception.
Story concerns storytelling, repentance, and a bishop's return to Judaism; no theme of lust/desire (תאוה).
No study hall setting; scenes occur in Besht's home, a wealthy man's house/synagogue, and a city square.
The Baal Shem Tov prophesies that David will know himself when his exile ends, and this prediction comes true when heavenly voices reveal his identity.

Story explicitly depicts fear: the boy weeps in fear after the Maggid's rebuke, the Baal Shem Tov tells him 'אל תפחד', followers 'חרדו מאוד' fearing R. Yaakov Yosef's punishment, and R. Mendel reassures people 'אל תפחדו'.
Story depicts a tzaddik's miraculous revelation of adultery via summoning the dead, not a prophecy/prediction that comes true.

The story explicitly narrates how R. Avraham Yehoshua Heshil of Apta was a hidden tzaddik unknown to the people of Yassi, and the miracle with the dead witness revealed his true stature ('then the people of Yassi knew who the Rav was').
Story depicts a rebbe exposing adultery in his community; no master-disciple relationship is portrayed.
The story depicts the rabbi and his wife (the rebbetzin) interacting as a couple, and centrally features the gvir and his unfaithful wife's marriage, ending in divorce.
Story takes place in the rabbi's home, the community council house, fishmongers' market, and synagogue — no beit midrash/yeshiva setting depicted.
Story is set in the rabbi's home and communal meeting house in Yassi, not a hasidic court (chatzer ha-tzaddik).
The living man trembles (ויחרד) with fear when summoned to judgment by the dead, fearing his impending death.
Story is about a halakhic/moral dispute adjudicated by the Maharsha, not about an outsider being drawn close to a tzaddik or Hasidism.
Story involves a rabbi (Maharsha) judging a case, not a Hasidic rebbe-disciple relationship.
The Maharsha designates a spot in the beit midrash for the judgment between the living man and the dead.
The Maggid of Kozhnitz is a central character explicitly titled as maggid (preacher).
The brother angrily rebukes the visitor, accusing him of mocking him and reminding him of his dead sister.
No explicit fear depicted; story centers on childlessness, broken engagement, and reconciliation.
The protagonist is already a devoted Hasid of the Maggid; no outsider/skeptic is drawn close.
No study hall setting; story takes place in homes, on the road, at a fair, and in an inn.
The tzaddik Gorde experiences a mystical revelation seeing the Maggid suspended three cubits in the air and overwhelming spiritual sweetness during prayer.
Story centrally involves the King of Prussia and his non-Jewish royal doctor who heals the Jewish protagonist.

The story depicts a clear miraculous healing — the dying man recovers without medicine after the doctor (accompanied by angel Rafael, per the Maggid's prophecy) enters, a discrete supernatural before/after event.
The Maggid of Mezeritch tells the visitor about the doctor and angel Raphael, a prediction that comes true months later when the king's doctor heals him without medicine.
The story depicts awe-inspiring revelation of the Baal Shem Tov's greatness — house filled with light, visible angels — evoking religious awe of the tzaddik.
Story depicts Rema's acceptance and installation as rabbi/leader of Krakow community.
Story concerns two pre-Hasidic rabbinic colleagues (Rema and R. Chaim) and Elijah, not a Hasidic rebbe-disciple relationship.
The story depicts Rabbi Chaim's marriage arrangement and his married life with the baker's daughter, noting she is a kosher wife who does her husband's will.
Story depicts study in a private house/room arranged by R. Chaim, courtroom, and baker's house—not a beit midrash or yeshiva setting.
Story concerns Rabbi Moshe Isserles and R. Chaim, a pre-Hasidic rabbinic setting; no hasidic rebbe's court depicted.
Story is the Baal Shem Tov's birth/childhood narrative; no interaction among Hasidim depicted.
The story narrates the Besht's origins and childhood; no rebbe-disciple relationship is depicted.
The story explicitly mentions the Baal Shem Tov becoming a guardian of the beit midrash, where he would study while others slept.
No depiction of a rebbe's court; settings are emperor's court, beit midrash, wilderness — pre-Besht biography.
Settings are a hut of seclusion, the rabbi's house, a village home, and a forest—no beit midrash/yeshiva depicted.
Shabbat is mentioned only incidentally (he came home on erev Shabbat); the story centers on the Besht's revelation, not Shabbat.
The tzaddik (Baal Shem Tov) appears, but his own family members do not; the wife and children mentioned belong to the poor scholar, not the tzaddik.
The Besht stays in Zaslav over several Shabbatot, and key action occurs 'after Shabbat in the middle of the day.'
The story explicitly attributes Shabtai Tzvi's fall to 'גדלות וכעס' (arrogance and anger), and depicts the Besht being humiliated/angered enough to accept death and vow revenge.
The Besht predicts the death of R. Eliezer, the fate of his wife, the killing of his opponents by Ishmaelites, and the printing of a heretical book—all of which come true.
The rabbi belatedly discovers the book on his table is the forbidden Sabbatean Chemdat HaYamim when the Besht recognizes it; also retrospective recognition of the cause of death (insult by two men).
Story explicitly centers on the Besht's Shabbat in Sharigrad and his Motzaei Shabbat revelations after Havdalah.
Story concerns repentance, storytelling, and a bishop's return to Judaism; no lust/desire theme.
No study hall setting; scenes occur in homes, roads, and a synagogue, not a beit midrash.
The story explicitly describes R. David's fear (ויחרד לב הרר״ד... כי הוא ירא) regarding his standing before the Baal Shem Tov.
Story explicitly illustrates virtues (humility, self-blame, acceptance of exile) and ends with explicit moral lesson about weighing reward against loss and not judging hastily.
The Baal Shem Tov reveals to R. David the hidden mystical reason (a 70-year-old soul awaiting tikkun) for his delay, and a heavenly voice/revelation discloses his innocence and the true culprit.
The story depicts conflict between R. David and the wicked preacher who frames him and orders him beaten.
The Baal Shem Tov reveals that a soul from 70 years ago was awaiting tikkun, and a heavenly voice ('from heaven they told me') knocks on windows calling R. David's name.
The Baal Shem Tov predicts that R. David will know on his own when his exile ends, which comes true.
The story shows discovery of an anonymous benefactor in real time (via the shamash watching), not a belated realization that someone was secretly righteous or had hidden identity.
The deceased's soul reports its resting place in Gan Eden among the righteous and describes being elevated to upper worlds, depicting heaven as a setting.
The Baal Shem Tov perceives a light over a hidden righteous woman, but the story centers on her humility and piety rather than a narrative of revealing a hidden tzaddik.
The Besht recognizes the woman's righteousness via the light, but this is immediate clairvoyant perception, not belated/retrospective recognition.
The new tax collector slanders/lies to the lord, falsely claiming the former collector went to a sorcerer to bewitch him.
Besht protects/saves a child from fatal illness through prayer and oath.
The Baal Shem Tov is described as scolding/raging at R' Leib for delaying on the road ('ורגז עליו').

R' Avraham Abba, who dismissed the Besht, comes to recognize his greatness when the Besht delivers the very Torah he heard from the Heavenly Prince of Torah — a revelation of the hidden tzaddik's stature.
The Baal Shem Tov states he heard the teaching in Gan Eden from heaven, and R' Avraham dreams of entering Gan Eden where the Sar HaTorah speaks — explicit communication with invisible beings.

The story reveals the hidden piety of R' Shabtai (a simple bookbinder) and foretells the birth of the Maggid of Kozhnitz, but the central revelation is of a hidden righteous act, not the giluy of a hidden tzaddik figure.
Story centers on the Besht with his disciples and a simple Jew, not interactions among Hasidim as peers.

The story narrates the revelation of the Baal Shem Tov as a true tzaddik to skeptical disciples (R' Zev Kitzes and R' David Forkes), who initially dismissed him but became his followers after the deceased disciple's posthumous testimony.
The Besht rebukes R. Michel's false humility and his stubborn refusal to admit defeat out of pride/shame, stripping his Torah as moral correction.
Story illustrates virtues (humility, honesty, Sabbath observance) — Besht rebukes false humility and pride/contentiousness in learning, transforming the scholar into a tzaddik.
The kloyz is mentioned and a beit midrash is referenced as a place the two men go, but no scene is actually set there; it is incidental.
R' Michel goes to the beit midrash to read shnayim mikra, and is found there when summoned.
The story depicts Rabbi Abba's awe/astonishment at the Baal Shem Tov's hidden knowledge ('נתבהל מאוד על ידיעתו מה שבסתר'), showing awe of the tzaddik.
Depicts a Hasidic master (Baal Shem Tov) and a layperson petitioner, not a rebbe-disciple relationship.
No beit midrash or yeshiva setting appears; scenes occur in homes, the road, a forest house, and Leipzig market.
The third brother is explicitly a mitnaged (opponent) of the Baal Shem Tov, depicting a tzaddik-vs-Jewish-opponent conflict that is resolved through healing.
The Baal Shem Tov prays at the sick brother's house on Erev Shabbat and Shabbat morning, with the healing occurring during Shabbat prayers.
Story involves anger and a miraculous healing/swap, but no explicit fear is depicted.
The nobleman who attempted to kill the Baal Shem Tov is punished — his hand withers and cannot be restored.
The Baal Shem Tov is attacked by a non-Jewish lord who draws his sword to kill him; clear tzaddik-vs-antagonist conflict.
Discrete miracle: Besht transfers paralysis from the sick woman (hands healed) to the attacking officer (his hand withers), with clear before/after.

A skeptical non-Hasidic wealthy scholar who never crossed the threshold of Hasidism travels to the Besht, is shown his sin via a revealed-knowledge tale, and falls before him in repentance — classic rapprochement arc.
The story depicts tzaddikim gathered at a yahrzeit but the narrative focus is on a Besht tale told to a non-Hasidic gvir; no meaningful interaction among Hasidim is depicted.
No beit midrash or yeshiva setting appears; scenes occur in homes, on the road, and at a tzaddik's residence.
The story centers on the Besht rescuing a woman; disciples are merely passive travel companions, not in a substantive master-disciple relationship.
No study hall setting appears; scenes occur in a house, on the road, and at a wedding.
The Baal Shem Tov is in direct conflict with a non-Jewish doctor, exposing him as a thief and shaming him.
No maggid/preacher character appears; the story features a rebbe (Besht), his disciple, and a merchant.
The merchant is already seeking the Besht for his wife's illness; the story centers on fulfilling a vow and the tzaddik's foresight/bitachon lesson, not on drawing a skeptic/outsider close.
No beit midrash or yeshiva setting depicted.
The story explicitly mentions the Angel of Death (מה"מ - מלאך המוות) fleeing from the Baal Shem Tov and then being given permission over him.
Story describes the Baal Shem Tov's death; no use of amulets, names, or practical kabbalistic operations depicted.

The story describes the Baal Shem Tov's death; while it mentions past struggles against the Sabbatean sect and a brief encounter with the angel of death, the narrative centers on his passing rather than depicting an actual conflict scene.
The story illustrates the tzaddik's virtues of scrupulous honesty (insisting on full forgiveness for the fine) and extreme piety through fasting, framed as 'praises' (שבחי).
Setting is a synagogue (beit knesset) for prayer, not a study hall; 'בבה״מ שלו' likely refers to his synagogue/place but the scene is communal prayer, not study.
No study hall setting; story occurs in homes, Hebron, Cave of Machpelah, and prison.
The innkeeper explicitly refuses to go to the lord out of fear of being beaten cruelly, and the townspeople fear missing their deadline — clear fear of danger.
No maggid/preacher character appears; the story features a rebbe and a wealthy man.
The protagonist is already a disciple sent by his rebbe to learn bitachon; no outsider/skeptic is drawn close to Hasidism.
Story centers on a rebbe sending a man to learn trust from a wealthy individual; no interaction among Hasidim as peers is depicted.
Story illustrates R. Leib Sarah's virtues: secretly supporting hidden tzaddikim and miraculous deeds at the Duke's court.
Story features hidden tzaddikim (נסתרים), 'עלמא דאיתכסיא', and miraculous kfitzat haderech — esoteric mystical knowledge and practices.
No maggid/preacher character appears; the story features a rebbe (R' Leib Sarah's) and a matchmaker.
No study hall setting; scenes occur in a village home, palace, and the rebbe's place.

The story explicitly invokes יראת שמים — citing 'וראו כל עמי הארץ כי שם ה' נקרא עליך... ויראו ממך' and depicting the officer overcome with fear/awe at the Rebbe's tefillin, plus awe before the tzaddik.
Story recounts persecution/arrest of R. Shneur Zalman and his interactions in prison; no outsider/skeptic is drawn close to a tzaddik.
This is a biographical/persecution narrative about R. Shneur Zalman's arrest and imprisonment, not a hidden-tzaddik revelation story.
Story focuses on R. Shneur Zalman's biography, arrest, and conflicts; no depiction of a marital relationship.
No beit midrash or yeshiva setting depicted; story involves study at home, travels, prison/fortress, and royal court.
Story focuses on R. Shneur Zalman's biography, conflicts, and imprisonment; no depiction of a hasidic court as a space.
The sister-in-law deceitfully feigned friendship while secretly smearing dove's blood on the woman's sheet.
The story depicts belated recognition that the sister-in-law was secretly an enemy planting dove's blood, and that the woman was actually pure — hidden truths revealed retrospectively.
Story depicts Hasidim collectively waiting, observing, and discussing among themselves (e.g., setting watch at the Rebbe's door, marveling together), beyond just master-disciple interaction.
The story depicts a heated dispute between two rabbis, with shouting ('צעק') and a quarrel, and concludes with a moral about not rushing to quarrel.
The story depicts the death of R. Baruch's daughter that year.
Story is about a tzaddik (Rabbi Aharon of Strashelye) rescuing a man from demons, not about revealing a hidden tzaddik.
Wife is mentioned briefly sitting by the sick husband, but the marital relationship itself is not depicted.
The story depicts the rebbe's court at Strashelye with hasidim waiting in the great house for the rebbe to emerge from his chamber.
The story explicitly describes the Shpoler Zeide bringing sinners of Shpole back to their Creator (teshuvah), turning Sabbath-violators into repentant Jews.
The Sabba of Spola foretells the husband's return date and appears prophetically to the husband in Danzig, clearly depicting supernatural knowledge/perception.
The story centers on two brothers and a parable; no depiction of broader Hasidic peer interaction beyond a brief mention of joining the Maggid's circle.
The Jewish protagonist becomes an arrendar/innkeeper (שכר לו האראנדא, called the ארינדאר/מוכסן throughout).
Story centers on a tzaddik's miraculous intervention and rectification of a past wrong (broken engagement) rather than illustrating a general virtue.
Story explicitly concerns the hitgalut of R. Yaakov Yitzhak of Lublin and a skeptic's recognition of his hidden knowledge.
The tzaddik of Lublin identifies and counters demons/spirits impersonating a family, a conflict between tzaddik and demonic antagonists.
The story explicitly narrates how the townspeople of Yassi did not know the rabbi was a hidden tzaddik until this miraculous revelation, ending with 'then the people of Yassi knew who the rabbi was.'
Story centers on a rebbe exposing an adulteress; no master-disciple relationship is depicted.
The story depicts the rabbi and his wife (the rebbetzin) as a married couple, and centrally involves the gvir and his adulterous wife, leading to divorce.
Story is set in rabbi's house, fishmongers' street, and community council hall — no beit midrash/yeshiva setting depicted.
The story includes interaction with the local non-Jewish lord (שר העיר) who summons the innkeeper, fitting the tag.
The rebbe investigates by ordinary observation and questioning, not supernatural perception; the innkeeper's power comes from bitachon, not from seeing/knowing supernaturally.
The bishop initially dismisses Rabbi Moshe as a mere youth unworthy of honor, but after the disputation belatedly recognizes his true greatness and wisdom.
Story depicts friendship between two rabbinic peers (Rema and R. Chaim), not a rebbe-disciple Hasidic relationship.
Study takes place in a private room in the baker's house, not a beit midrash or yeshiva.
Story is about a rabbi/av beit din and his friend, not a hasidic rebbe's court.
Story depicts an adulterous affair (a wealthy man fornicating with two married women in a cave) as the sin causing the plague.
Story depicts conflict between Jews and Ishmaelite/Christian antagonists (bishop inciting persecution), resolved through the tailor's clever scheme.
The tzaddik of Lublin confronts and dispels demons/shedim impersonating in-laws — a tzaddik-vs-demon conflict.
The story references the prior-incarnation man who died sixty years earlier, a depicted death event central to the plot.
The story is about the Baal Shem Tov's soul-ascent annulling a decree against Jews, not a prophecy/prediction that comes true.
Besht has an aliyat neshama in his sleep, perceives a heavenly decree against the Jews, and intercedes — clear supernatural perception.
Story is about love/devotion to God via a king parable; no sexual or bodily desire (תאוה) is depicted.
The story explicitly mentions the Rav's 'יראתו' (awe/fear) over potential bittul Torah, and depicts profound awe before tzaddikim whose oaths shake the heavenly court.
The chasid encounters R. Pinchas of Koretz before his fame, but the story's focus is the oath/blessing conflict between two tzaddikim, not a discovery/advent narrative.
The innkeeper initially denies/conceals his secret when asked ('ויכחד הרינדאר ויאמר לא ידעתי'), and the Rebbe disguises himself; both involve deception.
The wealthy man schemes to expose the rabbi as a fraud, accusing the Hasidim of false claims ('כחשם'/'שקר בפיכם') and hiring men to deceive him.
The Rebbe of Apta supernaturally discloses the woman's hidden sin upon her arrival — a giluy of concealed knowledge.
The 'בעל אכסניא' here is a host/lodging-keeper but the story centers on him as a hasid seeking advice; not depicted in a tavern-keeping role meaningfully, though innkeeper sense is plausible.
The story features a tzaddik advising a layman, not a master-disciple relationship.
No study hall setting; story takes place in homes, streets, palace, and court.
Story centers on hachnasat orchim and a foolish son, not the revelation of a hidden tzaddik.
Story centers on hospitality, brotherly bond, and a blessing fulfilled; no rebbe-disciple relationship depicted.
While a 'rosh yeshiva' role is mentioned, no scene actually takes place in a beit midrash/yeshiva setting.
Story concerns hospitality at a roadside inn and a rabbi/yeshiva head; no hasidic court depicted.
Story centers on a hidden tzaddik testing the Besht; no explicit depiction of yirah/awe of God or tzaddik as a theme.
Story is about a hidden tzaddik teaching Besht a lesson, not an inauguration.
Story centers on a hidden tzaddik's mystical rectification of a past sin, not on illustrating a virtue of the Besht.
Setting is a forest hut, not a study hall.
No study hall setting appears; scenes occur in homes, on roads, and at a tzadik's house.
No maggid/preacher character appears; the story features the Besht, his disciple R. David Pirkus, and a merchant.
No tavern-keeper or innkeeper appears; characters are a tzaddik, merchants, and a wealthy patron.
No study hall setting depicted; scenes occur in homes, on roads, in a forest, and at the Besht's room.
Story involves visits to the Besht but depicts travel/encounters, not the court space itself.
The story showcases the tzaddik's clairvoyance and prevents a fraudulent marriage, but does not depict an outsider/skeptic being drawn close to the tzaddik or Hasidism.
Story centers on a tzaddik, a wealthy follower, family conflict, and a fraudulent get; no depiction of Hasidim interacting with one another as such.
No beit midrash or yeshiva setting depicted; scenes occur in the rabbi's home, the wealthy man's house, and a rabbinical court.
No character is depicted as angry; the story centers on a tzaddik's blessing, confession, and miraculous rescue.
The lost travelers cry out in terror in the swamp, and the rescue is framed around fear/danger of death.
The story involves a false accusation against the peasant (theft of horses he didn't commit), but this is mentioned briefly as background; no character is depicted actively lying in the narrative.
Story features a tzaddik (R. Elimelech), not a maggid/preacher character.
The story centrally features a Jewish arendar/innkeeper (Arinda/Kretshme keeper) as a main character.
The lord raises his voice in anger at his wife, rebuking her wickedness; also her hateful incitement against the Jew reflects heated emotion.
The nobleman's heart trembles with fear ('הכה לבבו') upon entering the tzaddik's presence, depicting fear of the tzaddik.
The wife incites her husband with false accusations against the Jewish leaseholder, and a demonic spirit deceives the household by impersonating the lord for a year.
Story is about a pious Hasid's miraculous providence in marrying off his daughter; no skeptic/outsider is drawn close to a tzaddik.
Hasidim/חסידים actively assist R' Yeshaya — bringing provisions, lending vessels, urging him to seek matches — depicting interaction among Hasidim beyond the master-disciple bond.
Story narrates the Besht's family origins and childhood; no rebbe-disciple relationship depicted.
The story explicitly mentions the Besht serving as guardian of the beit midrash and the conduct of those sleeping/waking there.
While the story shows wonder at the Besht's powers, it does not explicitly depict yirah/awe of God or tzaddik as a thematic focus.
Story concerns mystical knowledge, miracles, and the Besht's hidden identity; no theme of lust/desire.

While the Besht is eventually recognized by his brother-in-law and robbers, the central narrative concerns the transmission of R' Adam's writings and the Besht's hidden life, not a focused revelation/giluy event.
The story predates the Besht's emergence as a rebbe; 'חצר הקיסר' refers to the emperor's court, not a hasidic court.
The story explicitly describes the Besht as 'ירא שמים' and depicts the rabbi rising in awe before the great light revealed from the Besht's face.
The story culminates in the Besht's revelation and the community formally accepting him as rebbe on a throne of branches.
The story explicitly narrates the Baal Shem Tov's giluy from hidden tzaddik to revealed leader, accepted as rabbi by the community.
The story illustrates the Baal Shem Tov's humility (hiding his greatness, serving guests anonymously) and piety (secluded prayer, ritual immersion), which are virtues of the tzaddik.
Settings include a forest hermitage, home, and the rabbi's house, but no beit midrash or yeshiva is depicted.
No clear act of lying or deception by a character; the story involves revelation, prayer, and healing.
The story vividly depicts trembling, awe, and Shekhinah-presence during the Baal Shem Tov's prayer, evoking yirah of God and the tzaddik.
Story concerns family/marriage matters among tzaddikim, not a rebbe-disciple relationship.
Story illustrates virtues of honest labor, simplicity, piety, and contentment in the craftsman.
The maggid (מוכיח) is a central character distinct from the rabbi and dayan.
The story depicts events during Shabbat prayer where the dayan curses the maggid.
The Besht is depicted as running a tavern (שיינק) in the village, with reference to a debt of yayin saraf.
The story explicitly mentions a Shabbat meal (פ"א שבת... ובסעודה) in the community.
R. Gershon and R. Leib Mokhiach perceive in dreams that a figure is a klipah vs. truly R. Aharon, and discover hidden coins/hairs confirming supernatural knowledge.
Story illustrates the Besht's faith/trustworthiness in keeping his word about the dowry and his miraculous provision, highlighting tzaddik virtues.
Besht sees a pillar of fire over a grave and prophesies that a man named R. David will be buried there, fulfilled 20+ years later — clear ruach hakodesh perception.
Story concerns childlessness and desire for children (תאוה לבנים in passing), not lust in the sexual/desire sense the tag denotes.
Story centers on a miraculous prophecy/encounter with Jonah ben Amittai, not on illustrating a general virtue.
No study hall setting; story takes place in homes, market, and forest house.
The Besht supernaturally knows what R. Nachman is doing/perceiving from afar, and R. Nachman has a divinely granted selective hearing — supernatural perception.
The lord boasts/glorifies his inn and then in rage attacks the Besht — pride/arrogance is depicted as a moral failing punished by paralysis.
The story illustrates the Besht's wisdom and his virtue of guiding a disciple away from unnecessary knowledge toward simple faith ('tamim tihyeh').
Story centers on repentance and rescue of a soul from apostasy; specific themes (teshuva, soul-rescue) rather than a general unspecified virtue.
The story is set in the beit midrash where the congregation gathered for Kol Nidrei.
The Besht's dispelling of strong wine via fear of God is a wondrous personal act, not a discrete miracle benefiting a recipient with clear before/after change.
The story shows the rabbi's recognition of the Besht's greatness but does not explicitly depict yirah/awe as a theme.
Story is about the Besht diagnosing a rabbi's spiritual flaw (heretical thoughts about tzaddikim), not about revealing a hidden tzaddik's identity.
The story explicitly centers on preparations for Shabbat at the rabbi's home.
The threat to burn the holy books is a background concern prompting heavenly advocacy, not a depicted conflict scene with an antagonist.
Story concerns imprisonment, release, and informers; no depiction of almsgiving (the 'nedavah' to the informer is incidental, not a charity theme).
The story explicitly describes the men fearing their judgment ('חרדו מאוד מאימת דינם').
Story recounts the Alter Rebbe's release from prison and informers' fates; no outsider/skeptic is drawn close to the tzaddik.
The Rebbe perceives that the figure passing the prison is not really Czar Pavel, and 30 days later this is confirmed by the Czar's assassination — a retrospective recognition of hidden truth.
The story concerns imprisonment, release, and informers; no study hall setting appears.
Story concerns the Rebbe's arrest, imprisonment, and release in Petersburg, not depictions of a hasidic court.
The merchant belatedly recognizes the rabbi was innocent and a scholar, and the rabbi himself retrospectively understands the Maggid sent him to receive the blow — clear retrospective recognition.
The story explicitly mentions planting 'אהבת תורה ויראת שמים' in disciples' hearts and praying 'בדחילו ורחימו,' directly invoking religious awe.
The episode with R. Yehoshua Zeitlin of Shklov depicts a Mitnagdic skeptic being won over by the Rav's learning, ending in his refusal to oppose Hasidism — a rapprochement arc.
The story explicitly illustrates the Rav's humility (hiding his greatness, not changing clothes, deferring to R. Menachem Mendel) and other virtues like devotion to his teacher and Torah greatness.
Mentions Maggid's house/beis and tzaddikim's locations incidentally, but does not depict the hasidic court as a setting.
The tzaddik saves a Jewish boy from forced conversion through his Torah discourses and miraculous wind, central protection narrative.
Story illustrates the tzaddik's virtue of saving a Jewish soul through his spiritual power and devotion.
The Hasidim retrospectively realize that the Rebbe's story 'from the past' actually referred to a present event happening that very night, recognizing the hidden meaning of his actions.
Hagiographic account of the Rebbe's wartime activities and death; no outsider/skeptic drawn close to Hasidism.
Narrative of the rebbe's wartime prayers and death; no belated recognition of a character's hidden identity or true nature.
Story concerns wartime flight, death, and burial of the rebbe; no depiction of a hasidic court.

The story includes the tzaddik R. Aharon being recognized/revealed as great by the Baal Shem/Maggid figures and a hidden encounter where his power is revealed posthumously, fitting giluy of a tzaddik.
The story centrally depicts the master-disciple relationship: R. Aharon serving Rabbenu 30 years, receiving smikha, and a disciple seeking salvation from his rebbe whose instructions protect him.
Wife is mentioned only incidentally (sitting by his bed during the affliction); no depiction of the marital relationship itself.
No beit midrash or yeshiva setting depicted; scenes occur in a tavern, the Rebbe's room/house, roads, and a grave site.
